book_claims.claim: Shrimp recommended as a high omega-3, omega-6, and vitamin B12 crustacean for the Longevity Diet
book_claims.mechanism: Lean protein source with omega-3 content and low mercury

book_claims.claim: Shrimp is used in the Longevity Diet (black rice with zucchini and shrimp); provides omega-3 (0.24 g EPA/DHA per 75 g cooked)
book_claims.mechanism: Lean protein and omega-3 source; low in mercury, suitable for frequent seafood servings

supplementary_research.finding: Shrimp consumption (PMID 11122711) raised LDL but simultaneously raised HDL and reduced triglycerides more than a low-fat diet; net cardiovascular effect is favorable compared to high saturated fat foods
Shrimp provides astaxanthin, selenium, and complete protein with very low caloric density (PMID 26950145); selenium supports antioxidant selenoprotein synthesis relevant to longevity
Shrimp is high in iodine, selenium, and vitamin B12 with a very lean protein profile (~24 g protein per 100 g cooked); low in saturated fat and mercury, making it an optimal longevity protein source

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Complete protein with all essential amino acids; high digestibility
Organic selenocysteine form; highly bioavailable; critical for antioxidant selenoproteins
Lower than fatty fish but still bioavailable marine omega-3s
Supports thyroid hormone synthesis; well absorbed from seafood

synergies.description: Black rice anthocyanins and shrimp astaxanthin provide complementary antioxidant protection; a Longevity Diet meal combination
Garlic allicin and shrimp selenium together activate antioxidant pathways more effectively than either alone
Vitamin C from lemon improves absorption of shrimp's non-heme iron component
